comments.text:
The 5x5 constraint is a good call, but I'd push back slightly on the "constant width makes programming easier" framing. Yes, it simplifies string length calculation, but you're paying a real cost in visual quality for characters like "i" and "l" that don't need the full width. The ZX Spectrum's designers made similar tradeoffs, but they had less choice.

That said, the practical argument here is sound. On an AVR128DA28 with 16KB RAM, you're already constrained to tiny displays. A 128x64 OLED is tight but workable, and 350 bytes for full ASCII is genuinely impressive. I've done similar work on STM32L0 chips, and every byte counts when you're trying to keep power consumption under 10mA.

One question: how does the lowercase letter optimization work? You mention drawing lowercase one pixel smaller, but the grid is 6x6 (with 5x5 usable). Are you actually reducing vertical height for ascenders/descenders, or is this more of a visual illusion from thinner strokes? If it's the former, the rendering code must have some branching logic based on case, which defeats part of the "constant width" benefit.

The lcamtuf attribution is correct. That 5x6 font has been forked into embedded projects for years. It'd be worth linking to the original if you haven't already, since people hunting for tiny fonts always search for it first.

kids.text:
I think you're spot-on about the visual cost, but I'd flip the framing slightly: the author isn't really arguing that constant width is universally better, just that it's a pragmatic choice for *this specific constraint*. On a 128x64 OLED you're already working with 8,192 pixels total. The math is going to be tight regardless.

That said, the "programming is easier" argument does gloss over something real. Variable-width fonts on microcontrollers aren't just harder to implement, they're harder to reason about when you're already juggling memory and display updates. I've done enough AVR projects to know that the cognitive load of tracking string widths at runtime, especially when your display code might be interrupt-driven, is a legitimate pain point that constant width eliminates.

The ZX Spectrum comparison is apt but maybe cuts the other way. The Spectrum used constant width because hardware bitmap rendering had no other option. Here, the author chose it deliberately despite having the option to vary widths. That's worth noting as a design decision rather than just a limitation inherited from the 80s.

If you're already dealing with the variable-width complexity, worth looking at what the embedded graphics community is using. The Adafruit GFX library has a few bitmap fonts in that size range, and there's also the work over at https://github.com/olikraus/u8g2 which lets you pick constant or variable width per font. u8g2 handles the layout math for you, which might save you from reinventing that part. That said, if 350 bytes is your constraint, rolling your own makes total sense.

I've actually shipped a couple of projects on AVR128DA with similar constraints. The constant width thing cuts both ways. Yes, it simplifies the math, but in practice I found myself working around it anyway, adding manual kerning tables for common pairs like "rn" or "cl" to avoid that chunky look. By that point you've lost the simplicity argument.

That said, if your constraint is "fit the whole font in 350 bytes," variable width becomes a space vs. complexity tradeoff that probably isn't worth it. The author's being pragmatic about the actual hardware limit, not claiming it's the theoretically optimal choice.

comments.text: I threw this on a STM32H7 with a 320x240 display and rendered the full ASCII set in about 2.4KB of flash. Legibility at arm's length is solid, though I had to bump to 6x6 for anything smaller than 10pt. The constant width is genuinely nice for UI layouts. https://github.com/verifyme/stm32-fonttest

kids.text: I did something similar on an ESP32 with a 240x135 display. The constant width was a lifesaver for a simple menu system - no kerning surprises, predictable layouts. I ended up going with 4x6 though because even 5x5 felt tight at 3 inches. Curious if you found any tricks for making descenders work without blowing up the line height.

Descenders are really the hard part at this size. I'd argue you're hitting the actual limit of what makes sense without getting into variable-height shenanigans.

The trick I've seen work is just accepting that descenders (g, j, p, q, y) sit on the baseline instead of dropping below. It costs you one row vertically but keeps everything in a predictable bounding box. On a 240x135 display especially, that trade is probably worth it - you're already pixel-starved.

If you really need true descenders, you're forced into either: (1) increasing line height past 6 pixels, which defeats the space savings, or (2) doing variable-width descenders that hang into the next line, which breaks your layout predictability. The second option is what bitmap fonts on old systems did, and it's a mess for UI code.

The 4x6 choice actually makes more sense than 5x5 for displays under 4 inches. The readability delta is minimal but you get a meaningful space win. I'd be curious what your actual use case is - are you rendering lots of text or mostly numeric/indicator displays?

comments.text:
I built a similar thing for a project displaying sensor data on an old 128x64 OLED display a few years back. The constant-width approach saved me hours of debugging layout code. I used a 3x5 font initially and kept running into issues with "M" and "W" looking like smashed garbage, plus kerning became a nightmare once you have variable widths and limited pixels.

The jump to 5x5 feels obvious in hindsight. You get lowercase distinction for almost free, and 350 bytes is trivial even on constrained hardware. I ended up burning about 400 bytes but added box-drawing characters for menu borders, which was worth it.

Only gotcha I'd mention: if you're doing this on a microcontroller with limited flash, make sure your bitmap encoding is actually optimal for your toolchain. I wasted a weekend using a naive C array before switching to a lookup table. Compiler wasn't optimizing the way I expected.

kids.text:
I find it interesting that embedded systems work keeps gravitating toward these micro-optimizations while projects targeting actual modern hardware are drowning in bloat. A 350-byte font is elegant, sure, but we're celebrating fitting readable text on a screen that costs $3 while web frameworks casually ship 2MB of JavaScript for a button.

The real insight here isn't the font itself, it's the constraint forcing good design decisions. Wish more projects were built with that mindset instead of "we have gigabytes, who cares."

lcamtuf's font is actually 5x6, not variable-width. The real win here is the monospace guarantee cutting layout code from hours to minutes. For OLED work under 160x128, this density is genuinely useful. Have you considered adding box-drawing characters for graphs?

> Have you considered adding box-drawing characters for graphs?

Yeah, but then you're really fighting the pixel budget. Box drawing for a 128x64 display ends up looking like a checkerboard from 2 feet away. I'd rather see someone build a small charting library that uses the existing 5x5 grid to fake smooth curves. The ZX Spectrum did this with block graphics and it actually looked decent.

comments.text: I built a weather display for an old 128x64 OLED using a similar 5x5 approach, and the memory savings were honestly surprising. The whole UI (time, temp, icons, 3-day forecast) fit in about 2KB of flash. Switched from a Raspberry Pi Zero to an ATtiny402 and the power draw dropped from 2W to 80mW. The readability at that size is totally fine if you accept the monospace constraint, which this font nails.

kids.text:
The 80mW figure sounds right for the ATtiny, but I'd push back on one thing: how are you handling the OLED refresh rate? Most 128x64 displays need 30-60Hz minimum to avoid flicker, and that's where power budgets get ugly fast. Are you doing partial updates, or refreshing the whole display each cycle? Because on paper ATtiny402 + OLED should be lower, but in practice I've seen refresh overhead eat most of that savings when you're not careful about SPI clock speed and update patterns.

The monospace constraint is real though. That's the actual win here-fixed-width lets you pre-calculate everything, no runtime string measurement. I'm curious what your 2KB includes: just the font bitmap, or the full UI state tree too?

comments.text:
I built a similar font for an embedded project on an STM32L152 a few years back, except I went 4x6 because I was trying to cram it onto a 96x64 LCD. The constant width thing was genuinely a lifesaver once I had to implement a simple menu system with right-aligned numbers. No second-guessing whether the "100" would clip.

The 5x5 approach here is smarter though. I spent way too much time hand-tuning kerning on a 4x6, and the letter distinctiveness problem (M, W) became real once you factor in sub-pixel rendering artifacts on cheap displays. Going one pixel bigger bought back legibility without the memory hit being significant. At 350 bytes, this would've fit in the flash of the chips I was working with three times over.

One thing I'd add: if you're targeting OLEDs, the constant-width property also makes animation smoother. Scrolling text doesn't jitter when every character is exactly 6 pixels wide.

kids.text: This takes me back to the mid-2000s when I was working on a graphing calculator project with a PIC microcontroller and a 128x64 LCD. We went 3x5 out of desperation, accepted it was basically unreadable, then discovered someone had already solved this problem in 1982. The constant-width constraint really is the killer feature once you're laying out menus or status displays. Wish I'd had something this well-thought-out back then.

I tested this on a Raspberry Pi Pico (2 MB flash, 264 KB RAM) with a 128x64 SSD1306 display. Renders at 60 fps with zero flicker. The 350 byte footprint is genuinely hard to beat. Only gotcha: antialiasing looks worse at this scale, so stick with 1-bit per pixel.
